sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=47, col=17).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Current level:
- World model: Diagonal chain of light gray dots connects three objects: white cross (top-left), purple square with magenta center (middle), dark gray cross (bottom-right). A ring of purple dots forms a diamond shape in the upper area. Gray borders surround the board.
- Goal model: Unknown - likely involves moving objects along chain or interacting with ring
- Recent findings: ['Chain connects three objects', 'Ring of purple dots is separate', 'Magenta center is unique color']
- Open questions: ['What does MOUSE do?', 'What is the goal?']
- Plan: Probe by clicking on purple square
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the changes clearly:
- White cross moved from [34,7] to [45,17]
- Purple square moved from [45,16] to [51,21]
- Magenta center moved from [47,17] to [53,22]
So clicking on the magenta center (47,17) caused all three objects on the chain to move down along the chain direction of the chain. The objects shifted down along the chain direction.
Let me understand the pattern:
- White cross: [34,7] -> [45,17] = +11 rows, +10 cols
- Purple square: [45,16] -> [51,21] = +6 rows, +5 cols
- Magenta: [47,17] -> [53,22] = +6 rows, +5 cols
So the objects moved down along the chain direction. The white cross moved more than the purple square and magenta center.
It seems like clicking on the magenta center causes all three objects to move down the chain. The white cross moved 11 rows down, the purple square moved 6 rows down.
Let me think about what the goal might be... The objects are on a diagonal chain. Clicking on the magenta center moves them all down along the chain. Maybe the goal is to move them to specific positions, or to get them to overlap with the ring of purple dots?
Looking at the ring of purple dots - it's at around (18-24, 36-42). The objects are now at (45,17), (51,21), (53,22). They're moving away from the ring.
Wait, maybe I should click on different parts of the chain to move the other direction? Or maybe I should click on the white cross to move them up?
Let me try clicking on the white cross now at [45,17] to see if it moves them in the opposite direction.
Actually, wait - let me re-examine. The white cross was at [34,7], and it moved to [45,17]. The purple was at [45,16], and it moved to [51,21]. The magenta was at [47,17], and it moved to [53,22].
So they all moved down-right along the chain. The white moved 11 rows and the purple/magenta moved 6 rows, 5 cols.
Now the white is at [45,17], purple at [51,21], magenta at [53,22].
Let me try clicking on the white cross at [45,17] to see if it moves them up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on white cross at new position [45, 17]
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 45, 'col': 17}])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Clicking on magenta center moves all chain objects down-right along chain (VERIFIED step 2)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"W": "white cross shape (12 px)", "g": "gray border", "B": "black background", "p": "purple dots", "w": "light gray chain dots", "M": "magenta center", "G": "dark gray cross"}, "hud_map": {}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}, "world_model": "Chain objects: white cross, purple square, magenta center. They move down-right when magenta is clicked. Ring of purple dots at (18-24, 36-42). Gray borders.", "goal_model": "Move chain objects to match ring position?", "recent_findings": ["Clicking magenta center moves all chain objects down-right", "White cross moved 11 rows, 10 cols", "Purple square moved 6 rows, 5 cols", "Magenta center moved 6 rows, 5 cols"], "open_questions": ["What is the goal?","Does clicking white cross move them up?","Does clicking purple square move them up?","Does clicking purple square move them up?","What does clicking on ring do?"], "current_plan": "Click white cross to see if it moves objects move up", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
